column2: Lung adenocarcinoma in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often nodular or polypoid masses with a variably dense, heterogeneous appearance. These lesions may have a ground-glass opacity or appear as solid areas with a mixed pattern of solid and ground-glass components. The tumor cells are often arranged in acinar, papillary, or solid patterns, with a background of fibrovascular stroma. Nodules may have a central area of necrosis or hemorrhage, and the borders can be well-defined or poorly defined. The nuclei of the tumor cells are generally enlarged, with pleomorphism, and may exhibit atypia, including increased nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio, hyperchromasia, and irregular nuclear contours. Mitotic activity is variable but can be observed. Capillaries and small blood vessels are commonly present within the tumor, sometimes forming vascular plexuses or nests. Additionally, there may be associated reactive changes such as fibrosis, inflammation, and hemorrhage.

Lung adenocarcinoma in a whole slide image typically presents as irregular, often nodular or polypoid masses with a variably dense, heterogeneous appearance. These lesions may have a ground-glass opacity or appear as solid areas with a mixed pattern of solid and ground-glass opacities. The tumor cells are often arranged in acinar, papillary, or solid patterns, with a background of fibrovascular stroma. Nodules may contain areas of hemorrhage, necrosis, and calcification. The cellular component is characterized by pleomorphic epithelial cells with irregular nuclear contours, prominent nucleoli, and frequent mitotic figures. The nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio is high, and there may be areas of mucin production, which can give the tissue a frothy or bubbly appearance. The margins of the tumor are often poorly defined, and the surrounding lung parenchyma may show signs of reactive changes such as fibrosis and inflammation.
